skill.name: debugging
skill.description: Structured debugging guidance for errors, test failures, logs, stack traces, production incidents, and root cause analysis.
skill.summary: Guides systematic debugging, log analysis, root cause discovery, fixes, verification, and incident postmortems.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis over-reported markdown backticks, example error strings, and template placeholders as command execution, weak crypto, sensitive data, and prompt injection. Human review found no confirmed malicious intent, network exfiltration, or prompt injection, but the bundled log parser reads user-supplied local files and may expose sensitive log content if misused.

medium_findings.title: Local Log Parser Can Read Sensitive Files
medium_findings.description: The helper script accepts a file path argument, reads that local file, and prints matched log lines plus analysis output. This is intended for debugging logs, but users could expose secrets if they point it at sensitive files or logs containing credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly reads a caller-supplied path with read_text and includes source file details and extracted line content in output. The behavior is legitimate for log analysis, so risk depends on what file the user supplies.

medium_findings.title: Command-Line Debugging Examples Require User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run local Python and grep commands against application logs. These examples are useful, but they can process sensitive logs and should be run only on approved files in trusted environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and intentional, but they are standard diagnostic commands rather than hidden execution. The concern is operational data exposure, not command injection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts are markdown code fences, inline error examples, SQL snippets, or JavaScript examples. I found no Ruby-style shell backtick execution in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples inside markdown fences or templates. They are not executable Ruby backticks and do not show command injection logic.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Fetch Calls Are Race Condition Examples
low_findings.description: The fetch calls are illustrative JavaScript snippets explaining asynchronous race conditions. They use relative API paths and do not send credentials or data to an external domain.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding section labels the code as a problematic race condition pattern. No external host, credential access, or exfiltration behavior appears in the cited lines.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Incident Template Placeholders
low_findings.description: The sensitive data, weak crypto, and prompt injection alerts map to incident template placeholders and sample incident identifiers. No prompt override text, Windows SAM path, or weak cryptographic implementation was found there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are table placeholders for affected systems and related incidents. They do not contain executable code, cryptographic logic, or instruction text aimed at overriding the evaluator.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Supplied Path File Reading
dangerous_patterns.description: The parser reads the path supplied on the command line and emits extracted content. This is acceptable for a debugging tool when users restrict input to approved logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file read and output behavior are direct and visible in the helper script. The pattern is risky mainly when sensitive files are selected.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes local command examples for parsing and searching logs. These should be treated as user-approved diagnostic commands, not autonomous execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are shown as examples and appear relevant to debugging. Users should still review file paths and redaction needs before running them.

content.value_statement: Bugs and incidents often need structured evidence, logs, and reproduction steps. This skill guides root cause analysis, log review, fixes, and postmortems.
actual_capabilities: Analyze error messages, exceptions, and stack traces across common languages.
Guide systematic reproduction, isolation, hypothesis testing, and verification steps.
Parse local log files with the included Python helper script.
Identify common patterns such as timeouts, race conditions, memory leaks, and null reference errors.
Structure incident postmortems with impact, timeline, root cause, and action items.
limitations: Requires relevant logs, error messages, code context, or reproduction steps.
Cannot prove a root cause without enough runtime evidence.
The helper script reads local files selected by the user and may expose sensitive log data.
Examples and templates may need adaptation for each stack and incident process.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Application Errors
use_cases.description: Turn an exception, stack trace, and related code into a focused root cause investigation and fix plan.

use_cases.title: Investigate Test Failures
use_cases.description: Compare expected behavior, failing output, fixtures, and recent changes to isolate why a test broke.

use_cases.title: Review Production Incidents
use_cases.description: Analyze logs, timelines, impact, mitigations, and follow-up actions for an operational incident.

prompt_templates.title: Explain This Error
prompt_templates.prompt: Debug this error message. Explain the likely cause, what evidence to check, and the safest next step: [paste error].

prompt_templates.title: Find a Test Failure Cause
prompt_templates.prompt: Investigate this failing test. Use the failure output, expected behavior, test setup, and recent code changes to identify the root cause.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Production Logs
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these production logs for recurring errors, timing patterns, affected services, likely root causes, and immediate mitigations.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Incident Postmortem
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a postmortem from this incident timeline, impact data, mitigation notes, logs, and action items. Separate evidence from assumptions.

output_examples.input: A developer provides a TypeError and the surrounding service logic.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies the missing null check, explains why the value can be absent, proposes a minimal fix, and lists verification tests.

output_examples.input: An on-call engineer provides logs with repeated database connection failures.
output_examples.output: The skill groups the errors by time, links the spike to likely connection pool pressure, and recommends mitigation and monitoring checks.

output_examples.input: A team provides an incident timeline after a service outage.
output_examples.output: The skill drafts a clear postmortem with impact, timeline, root cause, contributing factors, and owned action items.

best_practices: Provide exact errors, stack traces, timestamps, recent changes, and expected behavior.
Redact credentials, tokens, personal data, and customer data before sharing logs.
Verify each proposed fix with the original reproduction case and regression tests.
anti_patterns: Do not paste large logs without timestamps, service names, or a clear symptom.
Do not accept a root cause that is not tied to evidence.
Do not run helper scripts on sensitive files unless the output is approved for review.

faq.question: What kinds of issues does this skill help debug?
faq.answer: It helps with exceptions, test failures, stack traces, production incidents, logs, performance symptoms, and recurring error patterns.

faq.question: Does it include executable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Python log parser that reads a local file path provided by the user and prints extracted error data.

faq.question: Can it debug without logs or code context?
faq.answer: It can suggest likely causes, but reliable diagnosis needs concrete evidence such as logs, stack traces, code, or reproduction steps.

faq.question: Is it safe for production incident data?
faq.answer: Use it only with approved data. Redact secrets, tokens, personal data, and customer information before sharing logs.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: No confirmed network behavior was found. The fetch examples are documentation snippets about asynchronous race conditions.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
